items.q: What is the worst-case complexity of bubble sort?
items.misconception: O(n) because it just passes through the list.
items.why: Students count one pass and forget that bubble sort does up to n passes, each comparing up to n elements. The nested loop structure gives n times n = n squared.

items.q: What does it mean that RAM is volatile?
items.correct: Its contents are lost when power is removed.
items.misconception: It can explode or be damaged easily.
items.why: Students take 'volatile' from chemistry literally. In computing, volatile means contents don't persist without power - that's why RAM is used for working memory, not storage.

items.q: What does in-order traversal of a binary search tree produce?
items.misconception: Nodes in the order they were inserted.
items.why: Students forget the BST property: left subtree less than node less than right subtree. In-order (left, node, right) therefore visits values in sorted order - regardless of insertion order.
Correct answer: Kernel. “Shell” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which software lets the OS talk to a printer?
items.why: Correct answer: Driver. “Compiler” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many users can a single-user OS support at once?
items.why: Exact answer: 1 user. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
